AI1.tagline: Build, ground, and guard production LLM agents
AI1.subtitle: the mathematical theory of attention, autoregressive language models, and tool-using agents
foundations: the transformer and self-attention
autoregressive next-token prediction and the chain rule of probability
cross-entropy loss and backpropagation through the network
in-context learning and the conditioning of token distributions
dense embeddings and approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval
the ReAct reasoning and acting loop for agents
neural scaling laws relating loss to parameters, data, and compute

literature.title: Attention Is All You Need
literature.authors: Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin

literature.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
literature.authors: Devlin, Chang, Lee, Toutanova

literature.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
literature.authors: Lewis, Perez, Piktus, Petroni, Karpukhin, Goyal, Kuttler, Lewis, Yih, Rocktaschel, Riedel, Kiela

literature.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
literature.authors: Yao, Zhao, Yu, Du, Shafran, Narasimhan, Cao

literature.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
literature.authors: Kaplan, McCandlish, Henighan, Brown, Chess, Child, Gray, Radford, Wu, Amodei

AI1.build: Built a grounded, tool-using LLM agent in Python with PyTorch, Hugging Face Transformers, LangChain, and FAISS, adding retrieval-augmented generation, a ReAct planning loop, safety guardrails, and an LLM-as-judge evaluation suite.
objectives: Derive the transformer architecture from scaled dot-product attention, positional encoding, and residual streams
Explain self-attention, multi-head attention, and the softmax-based information routing mathematically
Analyze autoregressive next-token prediction, cross-entropy loss, and the backpropagation that trains it
Engineer prompts using zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought reasoning patterns
Implement retrieval-augmented generation with embeddings, vector search, and grounded context injection
Construct tool-using agents with the ReAct reasoning and acting loop
Design evaluation harnesses with task metrics, LLM-as-judge, and regression suites
Apply guardrails for prompt injection, hallucination, and output safety filtering
Quantify decoding strategies including temperature, top-k, top-p, and beam search
Deploy an agent with logging, tracing, and cost-aware orchestration

weeks.title: From language modeling to transformers
weeks.lecture: We formalize the language modeling objective, the chain rule of probability over tokens, and tokenization, then motivate why recurrence gives way to attention.
weeks.practice: Set up the project repo, load a pretrained model from Hugging Face, and run greedy and sampled generation.
weeks.project: Initialize the team chatbot scaffold with a baseline LLM and a chat loop.

links.title: Let's build GPT: from scratch, in code (Karpathy)

weeks.title: Attention and the transformer block
weeks.lecture: We derive scaled dot-product attention, multi-head attention, positional encodings, and the full encoder-decoder block with residuals and layer norm.
weeks.practice: Implement a single attention head and a transformer block in PyTorch and verify shapes against a reference.
weeks.project: Add a from-scratch attention visualization tool to the chatbot for explainability.

links.title: CS224N Lecture 7: Attention and LLM Intro

weeks.title: Training, scaling, and emergent behavior
weeks.lecture: We cover pretraining, cross-entropy loss, backpropagation through the transformer, and scaling laws relating loss to parameters, data, and compute.
weeks.practice: Fine-tune a small model with LoRA on a domain corpus and chart the loss curve.
weeks.project: Specialize the chatbot to a chosen domain via lightweight fine-tuning.

links.title: CS224N Lecture 3: Backpropagation and Neural Networks

weeks.title: Prompt engineering and in-context learning
weeks.lecture: We analyze in-context learning, few-shot demonstrations, chain-of-thought, and the theory of why prompts steer conditional distributions.
weeks.practice: Build a prompt-template library and run systematic A/B comparisons on a task set.
weeks.project: Add structured prompting and few-shot exemplars to improve answer quality.

weeks.title: Embeddings and vector retrieval
weeks.lecture: We study dense embeddings, cosine similarity, approximate nearest-neighbor indexing, and the bias-variance trade-offs of chunking.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its project specification, target domain, and evaluation plan.
weeks.project: Lock the project specification and stand up an embedding index over the knowledge base.

weeks.title: Retrieval-augmented generation
weeks.lecture: We formalize RAG as marginalizing over retrieved passages, and cover reranking, context-window budgeting, and grounding faithfulness.
weeks.practice: Wire a retriever to the generator and measure grounded versus ungrounded answer accuracy.
weeks.project: Convert the chatbot into a grounded RAG assistant over your corpus.

links.title: Stanford CS25: Retrieval Augmented Language Models

weeks.title: Tool use and function calling
weeks.lecture: We cover structured output, JSON schemas, function calling, and how tool results extend a model beyond its parametric knowledge.
weeks.practice: Expose calculator, search, and code tools and let the model invoke them via function calling.
weeks.project: Give the assistant tool access so it can act, not just answer.

weeks.title: Agentic loops and planning
weeks.lecture: We derive the ReAct loop, reasoning-acting interleaving, planning, memory, and multi-step task decomposition.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the agent executing a multi-step task end to end.
weeks.project: Upgrade to a ReAct agent that plans and chains tool calls toward a goal.

links.title: Stanford CS25: Generalist Agents in Open-Ended Worlds

weeks.title: Memory, multi-agent, and orchestration
weeks.lecture: We study short and long-term memory, agent state, and coordination patterns across multiple specialized agents.
weeks.practice: Add persistent memory and a second specialized agent that collaborates with the first.
weeks.project: Introduce memory and a helper agent to handle complex requests.

weeks.title: Evaluation of LLM systems
weeks.lecture: We cover task metrics, reference-based and reference-free evaluation, LLM-as-judge, and the statistics of small-sample comparisons.
weeks.practice: Build an automated eval suite with a labeled set and an LLM-judge scorer.
weeks.project: Add a regression test suite that scores every agent change.

weeks.title: Safety, alignment, and guardrails
weeks.lecture: We cover RLHF and preference optimization conceptually, prompt injection, jailbreaks, and content filtering.
weeks.practice: Red-team the agent for injection and add input and output guardrails.
weeks.project: Harden the agent with safety filters and injection defenses.

links.title: Stanford CS25: Aligning Open Language Models

weeks.title: Serving, cost, and observability
weeks.lecture: We cover quantization, KV-cache, batching, latency-throughput trade-offs, tracing, and cost accounting.
weeks.practice: Add tracing and cost logging, then optimize latency with caching and batching.
weeks.project: Make the agent observable, cost-aware, and ready to serve.

links.title: Stanford CS25: Serving Transformers, Lessons from the Trenches

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize the full stack from attention theory to deployed agentic systems and survey open research directions.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with live evaluation and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete grounded, tool-using, guarded agent with evaluation results.

project.summary: Teams build one production-grade LLM agent over the term, growing from a bare chat loop into a grounded, tool-using, guarded, and observable system. Each week adds a capability backed by the theory taught that week, and the agent is continuously evaluated on a held-out task set.

tools: PyTorch: tensors, autograd, and model implementation
Hugging Face Transformers: pretrained models and tokenizers
Hugging Face PEFT: LoRA and parameter-efficient fine-tuning
LangChain: agent orchestration and tool wiring
Ollama: local model serving for development
OpenAI API: hosted frontier models and function calling
Pydantic: structured output and schema validation

references.title: Attention Is All You Need
references.authors: Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin

references.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
references.authors: Lewis, Perez, Piktus, Petroni, Karpukhin, Goyal, Kuttler, Lewis, Yih, Rocktaschel, Riedel, Kiela

references.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
references.authors: Yao, Zhao, Yu, Du, Shafran, Narasimhan, Cao

references.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
references.authors: Kaplan, McCandlish, Henighan, Brown, Chess, Child, Gray, Radford, Wu, Amodei

references.title: Speech and Language Processing, 3rd edition draft

AI1.ai_usage: Students lean on AI coding assistants throughout, generating and refactoring the PyTorch attention blocks, LangChain wiring, and FAISS retrieval code, then vibe-coding their way from a bare chat loop to a full agent. They use AI to scaffold ReAct tool definitions and MCP server connections, to synthesize few-shot exemplars and labeled evaluation sets, and to draft LLM-as-judge rubrics. AI assistants also help interpret tracing logs and red-team transcripts, turning raw eval output into diagnoses of why an agent hallucinated or failed an injection test.

AI2.tagline: Forecast, decide, and act under uncertainty over time
AI2.subtitle: the probabilistic theory of time series forecasting and sequential decision making
foundations: stochastic processes, stationarity, and autocorrelation
ARIMA and exponential smoothing with maximum-likelihood estimation
recurrent networks and backpropagation through time
Markov decision processes and the Bellman optimality equations
temporal-difference learning and the policy-gradient theorem
multi-armed bandits, regret bounds, and Thompson sampling
Bayesian decision theory and expected-utility maximization

literature.title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
literature.authors: Mnih, Kavukcuoglu, Silver, Graves, Antonoglou, Wierstra, Riedmiller

literature.title: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
literature.authors: Schulman, Wolski, Dhariwal, Radford, Klimov

literature.title: Continuous control with deep reinforcement learning
literature.authors: Lillicrap, Hunt, Pritzel, Heess, Erez, Tassa, Silver, Wierstra

literature.title: Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning
literature.authors: Mnih, Badia, Mirza, Graves, Lillicrap, Harley, Silver, Kavukcuoglu

literature.title: Temporal Fusion Transformers for Interpretable Multi-horizon Time Series Forecasting
literature.authors: Lim, Arik, Loeff, Pfister

AI2.build: Built an end-to-end forecasting and control agent in Python with PyTorch, statsmodels, and Gymnasium, coupling a neural sequence forecaster to a deep reinforcement-learning controller trained with DQN and PPO inside a simulated environment.
objectives: Formalize stochastic processes, stationarity, and autocorrelation for temporal data
Derive classical forecasting models including ARIMA and exponential smoothing
Build sequence models with RNNs, LSTMs, and temporal transformers
Define Markov decision processes with states, actions, rewards, and discounting
Derive the Bellman equations and prove value and policy iteration convergence
Implement value-based and policy-gradient reinforcement learning algorithms
Quantify uncertainty with predictive intervals and Bayesian decision theory
Evaluate forecasts and policies with proper scoring rules and backtesting
Apply exploration-exploitation strategies including epsilon-greedy and bandits
Deploy a sequential decision agent in a simulated environment

weeks.title: Time series and stochastic processes
weeks.lecture: We define stochastic processes, stationarity, autocorrelation, and the decomposition of series into trend, seasonality, and noise.
weeks.practice: Load real time-series data, plot ACF and PACF, and test for stationarity.
weeks.project: Frame the running forecasting-and-control problem and load the target dataset.

links.title: Time Series Talk: Autocorrelation and PACF

weeks.title: Classical forecasting
weeks.lecture: We derive autoregressive, moving-average, ARIMA, and exponential smoothing models and the maximum-likelihood estimation behind them.
weeks.practice: Fit ARIMA and exponential smoothing baselines and backtest them.
weeks.project: Establish classical forecasting baselines for the project series.

weeks.title: Probabilistic forecasting and uncertainty
weeks.lecture: We cover predictive distributions, prediction intervals, proper scoring rules, and calibration.
weeks.practice: Produce probabilistic forecasts and score them with pinball and CRPS metrics.
weeks.project: Add calibrated uncertainty bands to the baseline forecasts.

weeks.title: Recurrent neural networks
weeks.lecture: We derive RNNs, backpropagation through time, vanishing gradients, and the LSTM and GRU gating that fix them.
weeks.practice: Train an LSTM forecaster and compare against the classical baselines.
weeks.project: Introduce a neural sequence forecaster to the project.

weeks.title: Temporal transformers
weeks.lecture: We adapt attention to sequences with causal masking and positional encoding for long-horizon forecasting.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its problem specification, dataset, and metrics.
weeks.project: Lock the specification and prototype a transformer-based forecaster.

links.title: Attention for Neural Networks, Clearly Explained (StatQuest)

weeks.title: Markov decision processes
weeks.lecture: We define MDPs, returns, discounting, value functions, and the Bellman optimality equations.
weeks.practice: Implement a gridworld MDP and solve it with value iteration.
weeks.project: Reframe the project as a sequential decision problem with an explicit MDP.

links.title: David Silver RL Lecture 2: Markov Decision Process

weeks.title: Dynamic programming and planning
weeks.lecture: We prove convergence of policy iteration and value iteration and discuss the contraction-mapping argument.
weeks.practice: Implement policy iteration and compare convergence with value iteration.
weeks.project: Compute an optimal planning policy for the known-model version of the task.

links.title: David Silver RL Lecture 3: Planning by Dynamic Programming

weeks.title: Model-free value methods
weeks.lecture: We derive Monte Carlo and temporal-difference learning, Q-learning, and the exploration-exploitation dilemma.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of a learned policy in the simulator.
weeks.project: Train a tabular or function-approximated Q-learning agent.

links.title: David Silver RL Lecture 4: Model-Free Prediction
David Silver RL Lecture 5: Model-Free Control

weeks.title: Deep reinforcement learning
weeks.lecture: We cover DQN, experience replay, target networks, and the deadly triad of function approximation.
weeks.practice: Implement a DQN agent and stabilize training with replay and target nets.
weeks.project: Upgrade the agent to deep value-based control on raw features.

weeks.title: Policy gradients
weeks.lecture: We derive the policy-gradient theorem, REINFORCE, advantage estimation, actor-critic, and PPO.
weeks.practice: Implement an actor-critic or PPO agent and compare sample efficiency.
weeks.project: Add a policy-gradient controller and benchmark it against the value agent.

links.title: David Silver RL Lecture 7: Policy Gradient Methods

weeks.title: Bandits and exploration
weeks.lecture: We cover multi-armed bandits, regret bounds, UCB, and Thompson sampling.
weeks.practice: Implement UCB and Thompson sampling and chart cumulative regret.
weeks.project: Add principled exploration to the decision agent.

links.title: David Silver RL Lecture 9: Exploration and Exploitation

weeks.title: Forecast-driven decisions
weeks.lecture: We combine forecasting and decision-making through Bayesian decision theory and expected-utility maximization.
weeks.practice: Couple the forecaster to the controller so predictions drive actions.
weeks.project: Integrate forecasting and control into one end-to-end agent.

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize probabilistic forecasting and sequential decision-making and survey open problems.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with backtests and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the integrated forecasting-and-control agent with evaluation results.

project.summary: Teams build one temporal decision agent that first forecasts a real time series and then acts on those forecasts inside a simulated environment. The project grows from classical baselines through neural sequence models into a reinforcement-learning controller, ending with an integrated forecast-driven decision policy.
ideas: Energy demand forecasting and storage control

tools: statsmodels: ARIMA and classical time-series models
Prophet: decomposable trend and seasonality forecasting

references.title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
references.authors: Mnih, Kavukcuoglu, Silver, Graves, Antonoglou, Wierstra, Riedmiller

AI2.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor the statsmodels ARIMA baselines, the LSTM and temporal-transformer forecasters, and the Gymnasium environment and reward code, vibe-coding the DQN and PPO training loops. They prompt AI to synthesize simulated episodes and edge-case series, to wire reward shaping and replay buffers, and to set up Optuna sweeps. AI also helps read backtest plots and learning curves, explaining why a policy diverged or a forecast lost calibration.

AI3.tagline: See the world with deep convolutional and transformer vision
AI3.subtitle: the representation-learning theory of convolutional and transformer models for vision
foundations: discrete convolution, receptive fields, and parameter sharing
backpropagation through convolutional and pooling layers
residual learning and skip connections in very deep networks
patch embeddings and self-attention for images
anchors, bounding-box regression, and non-max suppression
encoder-decoder networks and per-pixel segmentation loss
transfer learning and self-supervised pretraining

literature.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
literature.authors: He, Zhang, Ren, Sun

literature.title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
literature.authors: Dosovitskiy, Beyer, Kolesnikov, Weissenborn, Zhai, Unterthiner, Dehghani, Minderer, Heigold, Gelly, Uszkoreit, Houlsby

literature.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
literature.authors: Ronneberger, Fischer, Brox

literature.title: Mask R-CNN
literature.authors: He, Gkioxari, Dollar, Girshick

literature.title: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
literature.authors: Redmon, Divvala, Girshick, Farhadi

AI3.build: Built an end-to-end computer-vision pipeline in Python with PyTorch, torchvision, and Detectron2, progressing from a residual classifier to an object detector and a Mask R-CNN instance-segmentation system evaluated with mean average precision and intersection over union.
objectives: Derive the convolution operation, receptive fields, and parameter sharing from first principles
Explain backpropagation through convolutional and pooling layers
Build and train CNN architectures including residual networks
Adapt transformers to images with patch embeddings and self-attention
Implement object detection with anchors, region proposals, and bounding-box regression
Implement semantic and instance segmentation with encoder-decoder networks
Apply transfer learning and fine-tuning from pretrained backbones
Analyze representation learning, feature hierarchies, and learned invariances
Evaluate vision models with mean average precision and intersection over union
Deploy a vision pipeline with augmentation, training, and inference

weeks.title: Images, pixels, and learning
weeks.lecture: We cover image formation, color spaces, and the shift from hand-crafted features to learned representations.
weeks.practice: Load an image dataset, build a data pipeline, and train a linear classifier baseline.
weeks.project: Choose the vision task, dataset, and metrics for the running project.

links.title: UMich EECS 498 Lecture 1: Intro to DL for Computer Vision

weeks.title: Convolution and feature maps
weeks.lecture: We derive discrete convolution, kernels, stride, padding, receptive fields, and parameter sharing.
weeks.practice: Implement convolution and pooling in NumPy, then verify against PyTorch.
weeks.project: Build a small from-scratch CNN classifier for the project dataset.

weeks.title: Training deep CNNs
weeks.lecture: We cover backpropagation through convolutions, batch normalization, initialization, and optimization dynamics.
weeks.practice: Train a deeper CNN with augmentation and learning-rate scheduling.
weeks.project: Improve the baseline classifier with regularization and augmentation.

links.title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 4: Neural Networks and Backpropagation
UMich EECS 498 Lecture 10: Training Neural Networks Part 1

weeks.title: Residual and modern CNNs
weeks.lecture: We derive residual learning, skip connections, and why they ease optimization of very deep networks.
weeks.practice: Implement and train a ResNet and compare with the plain CNN.
weeks.project: Adopt a residual backbone for the project task.

weeks.title: Vision transformers
weeks.lecture: We adapt attention to images via patch embeddings, class tokens, and positional encoding, and contrast inductive biases with CNNs.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its task specification and evaluation plan.
weeks.project: Lock the specification and prototype a vision transformer backbone.

links.title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 8: Attention and Transformers

weeks.title: Pretraining and fine-tuning
weeks.lecture: We cover transfer learning, feature reuse, fine-tuning strategies, and self-supervised pretraining.
weeks.practice: Fine-tune a pretrained backbone and compare against training from scratch.
weeks.project: Switch the project to a pretrained, fine-tuned backbone.

weeks.title: Object detection foundations
weeks.lecture: We cover anchors, region proposals, bounding-box regression, non-max suppression, and the detection loss.
weeks.practice: Train a detector on the project images and inspect predicted boxes.
weeks.project: Extend the project from classification to object detection.

weeks.title: Two-stage and one-stage detectors
weeks.lecture: We contrast Faster R-CNN with single-stage detectors and analyze the speed-accuracy trade-off.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of detection results with mAP.
weeks.project: Benchmark a one-stage detector against the two-stage baseline.

links.title: UMich EECS 498 Lecture 16: Detection and Segmentation

weeks.title: Semantic segmentation
weeks.lecture: We derive encoder-decoder networks, the U-Net architecture, skip connections, and per-pixel loss.
weeks.practice: Train a U-Net for semantic segmentation and score with IoU.
weeks.project: Add pixel-level segmentation to the project pipeline.

links.title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 9: Detection, Segmentation, Visualizing
UMich EECS 498 Lecture 16: Detection and Segmentation

weeks.title: Instance segmentation
weeks.lecture: We cover Mask R-CNN, region-of-interest alignment, and joint detection and masking.
weeks.practice: Train Mask R-CNN and visualize instance masks.
weeks.project: Upgrade segmentation to instance-level masks.

links.title: UMich EECS 498 Lecture 16: Detection and Segmentation

weeks.title: Interpretability and invariance
weeks.lecture: We study learned feature hierarchies, saliency, class activation maps, and adversarial robustness.
weeks.practice: Generate saliency and CAM visualizations and probe adversarial examples.
weeks.project: Add interpretability visualizations to the project.

links.title: UMich EECS 498 Lecture 14: Visualizing and Understanding
Stanford CS231N Lecture 9: Detection, Segmentation, Visualizing

weeks.title: Optimization and serving
weeks.lecture: We cover quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, and inference latency.
weeks.practice: Optimize the model with quantization and export it for inference.
weeks.project: Make the vision pipeline fast and deployable.

links.title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 11: Large-Scale Distributed Training

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize convolutional and transformer vision and survey open research directions.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with metrics and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete detection-and-segmentation pipeline with results.

project.summary: Teams build one end-to-end computer-vision pipeline on a chosen image dataset, growing from a classifier into a detector and finally an instance-segmentation system. Each week adds capability backed by the convolution and representation theory taught that week, with continuous evaluation on held-out images.

tools: torchvision: datasets, transforms, and pretrained models
Hugging Face Transformers: vision transformer models

references.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
references.authors: Dosovitskiy, Beyer, Kolesnikov, Weissenborn, Zhai, Unterthiner, Dehghani, Minderer, Heigold, Gelly, Uszkoreit, Houlsby

references.title: U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications, 2nd edition

AI3.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor torchvision data pipelines, custom CNN and ViT modules, and Detectron2 and YOLO training configs, vibe-coding from a classifier up to a Mask R-CNN system. They prompt AI to build Albumentations augmentation stacks, synthesize annotation-format converters, and generate unit tests for IoU and mAP computation. AI also helps analyze confusion matrices, saliency maps, and failed detections, explaining why a model misclassifies or where boxes drift.

AI4.tagline: Train and serve AI at massive data and compute scale
AI4.subtitle: the algorithmic theory of distributed training, approximate, and streaming computation
foundations: Amdahl's law and the memory, compute, and communication walls
the MapReduce model, dataflow graphs, and fault tolerance
synchronous stochastic gradient descent and gradient all-reduce
model, pipeline, and tensor parallelism with ZeRO sharding
count-min sketch, HyperLogLog, and locality-sensitive hashing
the streaming model and the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma
neural scaling laws and compute-optimal resource allocation

literature.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
literature.authors: Kaplan, McCandlish, Henighan, Brown, Chess, Child, Gray, Radford, Wu, Amodei

literature.title: ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models
literature.authors: Rajbhandari, Rasley, Ruwase, He

literature.title: GPipe: Efficient Training of Giant Neural Networks using Pipeline Parallelism
literature.authors: Huang, Cheng, Bapna, Firat, Chen, Chen, Lee, Ngiam, Le, Wu, Chen

literature.title: Deep Speech 2: End-to-End Speech Recognition in English and Mandarin
literature.authors: Amodei, Anubhai, Battenberg, Case, Casper, Catanzaro, Chen, Chrzanowski, Coates, Diamos, et al.

AI4.build: Built a scalable training and serving pipeline in Python with PyTorch Distributed, DeepSpeed, Apache Spark, and Kafka, layering data and pipeline parallelism, ZeRO sharding, sketch-based approximate aggregation, and streaming ingestion measured for throughput and cost.
objectives: Analyze the complexity and communication costs of distributed algorithms
Derive data, model, and pipeline parallelism for large-scale training
Explain synchronous and asynchronous stochastic gradient descent and its convergence
Build big-data pipelines with MapReduce and dataflow frameworks
Implement approximate algorithms including sketches, hashing, and sampling
Design streaming algorithms with bounded memory over unbounded data
Quantify scaling laws relating model loss to data, parameters, and compute
Apply mixed precision, sharding, and gradient accumulation for memory efficiency
Evaluate throughput, latency, and cost trade-offs in distributed systems
Deploy a scalable training and serving pipeline on a cluster

weeks.title: Why scale and what breaks
weeks.lecture: We analyze the limits of single-machine computation, Amdahl's law, and the memory, compute, and communication walls.
weeks.practice: Profile a single-node training job and identify its bottlenecks.
weeks.project: Choose the scalable pipeline target and establish a single-node baseline.

weeks.title: Big-data frameworks
weeks.lecture: We cover the MapReduce model, dataflow graphs, partitioning, and fault tolerance.
weeks.practice: Implement a MapReduce-style aggregation and run it on a Spark cluster.
weeks.project: Build a distributed data-ingestion stage for the project.

weeks.title: Distributed storage and data formats
weeks.lecture: We cover columnar formats, sharding, partitioning, and the CAP trade-offs of data-intensive systems.
weeks.practice: Convert the dataset to a columnar format and benchmark scan throughput.
weeks.project: Optimize the project data layout for parallel access.

weeks.title: Data-parallel training
weeks.lecture: We derive synchronous SGD, gradient all-reduce, and the convergence effect of large batch sizes.
weeks.practice: Run data-parallel training across multiple workers and measure scaling efficiency.
weeks.project: Make the training stage data-parallel.

weeks.title: Model and pipeline parallelism
weeks.lecture: We cover model sharding, pipeline parallelism, tensor parallelism, and the bubble overhead they incur.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its scaling specification and target metrics.
weeks.project: Lock the specification and prototype a sharded model stage.

weeks.title: Memory and precision efficiency
weeks.lecture: We cover mixed precision, activation checkpointing, ZeRO sharding, and gradient accumulation.
weeks.practice: Apply mixed precision and sharding to fit a larger model in memory.
weeks.project: Scale the model size with memory-efficient training.

weeks.title: Sketches and hashing
weeks.lecture: We derive count-min sketch, HyperLogLog, and locality-sensitive hashing with their error bounds.
weeks.practice: Implement a count-min sketch and HyperLogLog and validate accuracy versus memory.
weeks.project: Add approximate aggregation to the data pipeline.

weeks.title: Sampling and dimensionality reduction
weeks.lecture: We cover reservoir sampling, random projections, and the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the scaled pipeline with throughput numbers.
weeks.project: Add sampling and random projection to reduce data volume.

weeks.title: Streaming algorithms
weeks.lecture: We define the streaming model, bounded memory over unbounded input, and windowed computation.
weeks.practice: Build a streaming aggregation over a simulated event stream.
weeks.project: Add a streaming ingestion path to the pipeline.

weeks.title: Online and approximate learning
weeks.lecture: We cover online gradient descent, regret bounds, and incremental model updates.
weeks.practice: Implement online learning that updates the model as data streams in.
weeks.project: Enable continuous online updates in the project.

weeks.title: Scaling laws and compute budgets
weeks.lecture: We derive empirical scaling laws relating loss to data, parameters, and compute, and compute-optimal allocation.
weeks.practice: Fit a small scaling-law curve from runs at several model sizes.
weeks.project: Use scaling-law analysis to choose the project compute budget.

weeks.title: Scalable inference and serving
weeks.lecture: We cover batching, caching, autoscaling, and latency-throughput-cost trade-offs at serving time.
weeks.practice: Deploy the model behind a scalable serving layer and load-test it.
weeks.project: Stand up scalable serving for the trained model.

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize distributed training, approximate and streaming algorithms, and scaling laws, and survey open problems.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with scaling benchmarks and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete scalable training-and-serving pipeline with benchmarks.

project.summary: Teams build one scalable AI pipeline that ingests, processes, trains on, and serves a large dataset, growing from a single node to a distributed cluster. The project layers in data parallelism, memory-efficient training, approximate and streaming algorithms, and scaling-law analysis, with throughput and cost measured at every stage.
ideas: Approximate near-duplicate detection at web scale

tools: PyTorch Distributed: data and model parallelism
DeepSpeed: ZeRO sharding and large-model training
Ray: distributed Python and ML workloads
NVIDIA NCCL: collective communication for GPUs
Weights and Biases: distributed experiment tracking

references.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
references.authors: Kaplan, McCandlish, Henighan, Brown, Chess, Child, Gray, Radford, Wu, Amodei

AI4.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor PyTorch Distributed and DeepSpeed launch scripts, Spark and Kafka pipeline code, and sketch-algorithm implementations, vibe-coding the all-reduce and ZeRO-sharded training stages. They prompt AI to write count-min sketch and HyperLogLog tests, synthesize streaming event data, and draft autoscaling and serving configs. AI also helps interpret throughput profiles, scaling-efficiency curves, and cost logs to locate the bottleneck a job hit.

AI5.tagline: Model and sample from data with deep generative models
AI5.subtitle: the probabilistic theory of variational, adversarial, autoregressive, and diffusion generative models
foundations: maximum-likelihood estimation of a data distribution
the evidence lower bound and variational inference
the reparameterization trick and the variational autoencoder
the adversarial minimax game and the optimal discriminator
the autoregressive likelihood factorization and causal masking
the diffusion forward and reverse denoising processes
score matching and stochastic differential equations

literature.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
literature.authors: Ho, Jain, Abbeel

literature.title: Generative Adversarial Networks
literature.authors: Goodfellow, Pouget-Abadie, Mirza, Xu, Warde-Farley, Ozair, Courville, Bengio

literature.title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
literature.authors: Kingma, Welling

literature.title: Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations
literature.authors: Song, Sohl-Dickstein, Kingma, Kumar, Ermon, Poole

literature.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
literature.authors: Rombach, Blattmann, Lorenz, Esser, Ommer

AI5.build: Built a conditional generative pipeline in Python with PyTorch and Hugging Face Diffusers, implementing and benchmarking VAE, GAN, autoregressive, and diffusion generators with classifier-free guidance and FID evaluation on a shared data modality.
objectives: Formalize generative modeling as learning a data distribution
Derive the variational lower bound and the VAE objective
Explain the adversarial minimax game and GAN training dynamics
Derive the diffusion forward and reverse processes and the denoising objective
Connect diffusion to score matching and stochastic differential equations
Build autoregressive generators and analyze their likelihood factorization
Implement conditional and guided generation including classifier-free guidance
Evaluate generative models with FID, likelihood, and sample-quality metrics
Analyze the likelihood, sampling, and mode-coverage trade-offs across model families
Deploy a conditional generative pipeline end to end

weeks.title: What is a generative model
weeks.lecture: We define generative modeling, maximum likelihood, latent variables, and the taxonomy of model families.
weeks.practice: Train a simple density estimator and sample from it.
weeks.project: Choose the data modality and generative task for the running project.

links.title: Stanford CS236 Lecture 4: Maximum Likelihood Learning

weeks.title: Latent variables and the ELBO
weeks.lecture: We derive latent-variable models, the evidence lower bound, and variational inference.
weeks.practice: Implement variational inference on a toy latent-variable model.
weeks.project: Establish a probabilistic baseline generator for the project data.

links.title: Berkeley CS294-158 Lecture 4: Latent Variable Models and VAEs

weeks.title: Variational autoencoders
weeks.lecture: We derive the VAE, the reparameterization trick, and the reconstruction-versus-KL trade-off.
weeks.practice: Train a VAE and explore its latent space by interpolation.
weeks.project: Build a VAE generator for the project modality.

weeks.title: Expressive and discrete latents
weeks.lecture: We cover hierarchical VAEs, vector-quantized latents, and posterior collapse.
weeks.practice: Train a VQ-VAE and inspect the learned discrete codebook.
weeks.project: Upgrade the generator with a discrete or hierarchical latent space.

links.title: Stanford CS236 Lecture 17: Discrete Latent Variable Models

weeks.title: Generative adversarial networks
weeks.lecture: We derive the GAN minimax objective, the optimal discriminator, and the Jensen-Shannon connection.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its generative specification and metrics.
weeks.project: Lock the specification and prototype a GAN generator.

weeks.title: Stabilizing GAN training
weeks.lecture: We cover mode collapse, Wasserstein GANs, gradient penalties, and training stability.
weeks.practice: Train a Wasserstein GAN and compare stability against the vanilla GAN.
weeks.project: Improve the GAN with stabilized training and conditioning.

weeks.title: Autoregressive generation
weeks.lecture: We derive the autoregressive likelihood factorization and causal masking for sequences and images.
weeks.practice: Train an autoregressive model and sample token by token.
weeks.project: Add an autoregressive generator and compare with the latent models.

weeks.title: Denoising diffusion
weeks.lecture: We derive the forward noising process, the reverse denoising process, and the simplified DDPM training objective.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of generated samples across model families.
weeks.project: Prototype a diffusion generator for the project data.

weeks.title: Score matching and SDEs
weeks.lecture: We connect diffusion to score matching and stochastic differential equations and derive the probability-flow ODE.
weeks.practice: Implement a score-based sampler and compare sampling schedules.
weeks.project: Refine the diffusion model with score-based sampling.

links.title: Stanford CS236 Lecture 16: Score-Based Diffusion Models

weeks.title: Guidance and conditioning
weeks.lecture: We cover conditional diffusion, classifier and classifier-free guidance, and latent diffusion.
weeks.practice: Add conditioning and classifier-free guidance to control generation.
weeks.project: Make the generator controllable and conditional.

weeks.title: Evaluating generative models
weeks.lecture: We cover likelihood, FID, inception score, precision-recall, and the difficulty of evaluating generation.
weeks.practice: Compute FID and precision-recall across the team's models.
weeks.project: Benchmark all project generators with shared metrics.

weeks.title: Efficient generation and serving
weeks.lecture: We cover sampling acceleration, distillation, and the quality-versus-speed trade-off at inference.
weeks.practice: Accelerate sampling with distillation or fewer steps and serve the model.
weeks.project: Make the conditional generator fast and deployable.

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize VAEs, GANs, autoregressive, and diffusion models and survey open research directions.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with samples, metrics, and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete conditional generative pipeline with evaluation results.

project.summary: Teams build one conditional generative system on a chosen data modality, implementing and comparing VAE, GAN, autoregressive, and diffusion approaches against shared metrics. The project culminates in a controllable, conditional generator backed by the probabilistic theory taught each week.

tools: Hugging Face Diffusers: diffusion model pipelines
Weights and Biases: experiment tracking and sample logging
einops: tensor reshaping for generative models

references.title: Generative Adversarial Networks
references.authors: Goodfellow, Pouget-Abadie, Mirza, Xu, Warde-Farley, Ozair, Courville, Bengio

references.title: Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations
references.authors: Song, Sohl-Dickstein, Kingma, Kumar, Ermon, Poole

references.title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models

AI5.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor PyTorch VAE, GAN, autoregressive, and Diffusers pipeline code, vibe-coding the DDPM noise schedule and classifier-free guidance. They prompt AI to synthesize toy datasets, write reparameterization and sampling routines, and generate tests for FID and precision-recall scoring. AI also helps read sample grids, latent interpolations, and FID curves, diagnosing mode collapse or posterior collapse from the evidence.

AI6.tagline: Perceive, plan, and act with embodied autonomous systems
AI6.subtitle: the estimation, planning, and control theory of embodied autonomous robots
foundations: rigid-body kinematics, transforms, and robot state representation
the Bayes filter and the recursive predict-update cycle
the Kalman filter and the particle filter for sensor fusion
configuration-space planning with A-star and rapidly-exploring random trees
PID control and the linear-quadratic regulator
the discrete-time Riccati equation and model-predictive control
policy-gradient reinforcement learning for continuous control

literature.title: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
literature.authors: Schulman, Wolski, Dhariwal, Radford, Klimov

literature.title: Continuous control with deep reinforcement learning
literature.authors: Lillicrap, Hunt, Pritzel, Heess, Erez, Tassa, Silver, Wierstra

literature.title: Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement Learning
literature.authors: Mnih, Badia, Mirza, Graves, Lillicrap, Harley, Silver, Kavukcuoglu

literature.title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
literature.authors: Mnih, Kavukcuoglu, Silver, Graves, Antonoglou, Wierstra, Riedmiller

AI6.build: Built a full perceive-plan-act autonomy stack in Python with ROS 2, PyBullet, and PyTorch, fusing sensors with Kalman and particle filters, planning with A-star and RRT, and tracking trajectories with model-predictive and reinforcement-learning controllers under sim-to-real domain randomization.
objectives: Derive rigid-body kinematics, transforms, and state representations for robots
Formalize probabilistic state estimation and the Bayes filter
Derive the Kalman filter and particle filter for sensor fusion
Implement motion planning with sampling-based and graph-search algorithms
Derive feedback control including PID and the linear-quadratic regulator
Apply reinforcement learning to continuous robot control
Build perception pipelines fusing camera, lidar, and inertial sensors
Analyze the sim-to-real gap and domain randomization strategies
Evaluate autonomy with task success, safety, and robustness metrics
Deploy an embodied agent in simulation with a real-transfer path

weeks.title: Robots, frames, and kinematics
weeks.lecture: We cover rigid-body transforms, coordinate frames, forward kinematics, and robot state representation.
weeks.practice: Set up a robot simulator and command joint and base motion.
weeks.project: Choose the embodied task and simulator for the running project.

weeks.title: Probabilistic state estimation
weeks.lecture: We derive the Bayes filter, the Markov assumption, and the recursive predict-update cycle.
weeks.practice: Implement a discrete Bayes filter for robot localization.
weeks.project: Add a basic state estimator to the project robot.

weeks.title: Kalman filtering
weeks.lecture: We derive the Kalman filter and its extended form for nonlinear Gaussian estimation.
weeks.practice: Implement a Kalman filter for fusing noisy position and velocity sensors.
weeks.project: Upgrade estimation with a Kalman filter for sensor fusion.

weeks.title: Particle filters and SLAM
weeks.lecture: We derive the particle filter, importance sampling, and the basics of simultaneous localization and mapping.
weeks.practice: Implement a particle filter for global localization in a known map.
weeks.project: Add robust localization to the project robot.

weeks.title: Perception and sensor fusion
weeks.lecture: We cover camera and lidar sensing, point clouds, calibration, and multi-sensor fusion.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: each team defends its task specification and metrics.
weeks.project: Lock the specification and build a perception stage fusing sensors.

links.title: ICP and Point Cloud Registration (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Graph-search motion planning
weeks.lecture: We derive configuration space, A-star, Dijkstra, and the heuristics behind optimal graph search.
weeks.practice: Implement A-star planning on an occupancy grid.
weeks.project: Add grid-based path planning to the robot.

links.title: Robot Motion Planning using A* (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Sampling-based planning
weeks.lecture: We cover RRT, RRT-star, probabilistic roadmaps, and planning in high-dimensional configuration spaces.
weeks.practice: Implement RRT and compare path quality with graph search.
weeks.project: Extend planning to sampling-based methods for complex spaces.

links.title: Sampling-Based Motion Planning 1/2 (Intro to Robotics L33)
Sampling-Based Motion Planning 2/2 (Intro to Robotics L34)

weeks.title: Feedback control
weeks.lecture: We derive PID control, stability, and the linear-quadratic regulator as optimal feedback.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: interim demo of the robot navigating with estimation and planning.
weeks.project: Add a feedback controller to track planned trajectories.

weeks.title: Optimal and model-predictive control
weeks.lecture: We derive the LQR, the discrete-time Riccati equation, and receding-horizon model-predictive control.
weeks.practice: Implement an MPC controller and compare with PID tracking.
weeks.project: Upgrade control with model-predictive trajectory tracking.

weeks.title: Reinforcement learning for control
weeks.lecture: We adapt policy-gradient and actor-critic methods to continuous action spaces for robot control.
weeks.practice: Train an RL policy for a locomotion or manipulation task in simulation.
weeks.project: Add a learned control policy to the robot.

weeks.title: Bridging the reality gap
weeks.lecture: We analyze the sim-to-real gap, domain randomization, system identification, and robustness.
weeks.practice: Apply domain randomization and test policy robustness across perturbed dynamics.
weeks.project: Make the learned policy robust to a sim-to-real transfer.

links.title: Berkeley CS287 Lecture 22: Sim2Real and Domain Randomization

weeks.title: Full autonomy stack
weeks.lecture: We integrate perception, estimation, planning, and control into one autonomy pipeline and discuss safety.
weeks.practice: Connect all modules into an end-to-end autonomous behavior.
weeks.project: Integrate the full perceive-plan-act stack on the robot.

links.title: MIT 6.843 Robotic Manipulation: Pick and Place

weeks.title: Final defense
weeks.lecture: We synthesize estimation, planning, and control theory and survey open problems in embodied AI.
weeks.practice: Team presentation: final demo with task metrics and an oral defense of design choices.
weeks.project: Deliver the complete autonomous embodied agent with evaluation results.

links.title: Russ Tedrake: Dexterous Manipulation with Diffusion Policies

project.summary: Teams build one autonomous embodied agent in simulation, layering perception, probabilistic estimation, motion planning, and control into a full perceive-plan-act stack. The project advances from classical estimation and planning into learned control and a sim-to-real robustness study, backed by the control and estimation theory taught each week.

tools: NVIDIA Isaac Sim: high-fidelity robot simulation
PyTorch: learned perception and control policies
AI6.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor ROS 2 nodes, PyBullet and MuJoCo environment code, and Kalman, particle-filter, A-star, RRT, and MPC implementations, vibe-coding the perceive-plan-act stack. They prompt AI to synthesize sensor-noise models and domain-randomization configs, wire message passing between modules, and generate tests for filter convergence and planner correctness. AI also helps analyze trajectory plots, estimation error, and sim-to-real failures to explain why a controller lost tracking.
